items.q: When an acid neutralises an alkali, what is the pH of the resulting solution?
items.correct: Approximately 7 if equivalent amounts of strong acid and strong alkali react — but it varies with strength.
items.misconception: Neutralisation always produces a solution with pH exactly 7 regardless of the acid and base used.
items.random_wrong: Neutralisation always produces a solution with pH 0.
items.why: Students learn the rule 'neutral = 7' so rigidly that they ignore weak-acid/weak-base cases where the salt is acidic or basic.

items.q: Why is the 4s orbital filled before the 3d orbital?
items.correct: Because the 4s orbital has a lower energy than 3d when both are empty, so electrons fill it first.
items.misconception: Because the 4s shell is closer to the nucleus than the 3d shell.
items.random_wrong: Because d orbitals can only fill after p orbitals in the next shell.
items.why: Students assume shell number directly correlates with distance/energy ordering, so 3-anything must come before 4-anything — but energy ordering crosses over at 4s/3d.

items.q: What is the difference between weathering and erosion?
items.correct: Weathering breaks rocks, erosion moves the pieces
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Weathering breaks rocks, erosion moves the pieces. “They are the same” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Freeze-thaw cracking is an example of:
items.why: Correct answer: Physical weathering. “Chemical weathering” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Water expands by about what percentage when it freezes (causing freeze-thaw)?
items.why: Exact answer: 9. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
